coredata.dc:title: Turn-on theranostic fluorescent nanoprobe by electrostatic self-assembly of carbon dots with doxorubicin for targeted cancer cell imaging, in vivo hyaluronidase analysis, and targeted drug delivery
coredata.dc:creator: Gao, Na
Yang, Wen
Nie, Hailiang
Gong, Yunqian
Jing, Jing
Gao, Loujun
Zhang, Xiaoling
coredata.dc:description: This paper reports a turn-on theranostic fluorescent nanoprobe P-CDs/HA-Dox obtained by electrostatic assembly of polyethylenimine (PEI)-modified carbon dots (P-CDs) and Hyaluronic acid (HA)-conjugated doxorubicin (Dox) for hyaluronidase (HAase) detection, self-targeted imaging and drug delivery. P-CDs/HA-Dox show weak emission in a physiological environment. By utilizing the high affinity of HA to CD44 receptors overexpressed on many cancer cells, P-CDs/HA-Dox are capable of targeting and penetrating into cancer cells, where they are activated by HAase. As a result, HA-Dox can be digested into small fragments, causing the release of Dox and thereby restoring the fluorescence of P-CDs. The theranostic fluorescent nanoprobe can effectively distinguish cancer cells from normal cells. The as-prepared nanoprobe achieves a sensitive assay of HAase with a detection limit of 0.65UmL−1. Furthermore, upon Dox release, the Dox could efficiently induce apoptosis in HeLa cells, as confirmed by MTT assay. The design of such a turn-on theranostic fluorescent probe provides a new strategy for self-targeted and image-guided chemotherapy.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Hyaluronic acid
Doxorubicin
Förster resonance energy transfer
Fluorescent nanoprobe
Targeted drug delivery

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The morphological characteristics of nanoparticles. TEM images of the (a) CDs, (b) P-CDs and (c) P-CDs/HA-Dox. (d) The Zeta potential values of CDs, P-CDs, HA-Dox and P-CDs/HA-Dox.
(a) UV–Vis absorption of CDs, P-CDs and P-CDs/HA-Dox. (b) Fluorescence (λex=310nm) emission spectra of P-CDs, P-CDs/HA-Dox and P-CDs/HA-Dox-HAase.

(a) Fluorescence emission spectra of P-CDs with successive additions of different amount of HA-Dox. (b) Plot of
F
0
F
− 1
in the linear range for P-CDs in the presence of HA-Dox (F0 and F were the emission intensities in the absence and presence of HA-Dox, respectively). (c) Emission spectra of P-CDs/HA-Dox composite with increasing concentration of HAase. (d) The linear plot of F–F0 versus HAase concentration in the range from 0 to 400U/mL.

Selectivity of the HA-Dox assembled P-CDs in PBS toward different substances. 1, P-CDs only; 2–5, NaCl, KCl, MgCl2 and NaH2PO4 (2.0mM, respectively); 6–8, ascorbic acid, glucose, urea (10mM, respectively); 9–12, glutathione, cysteine, homocysteine, histidine (2.0mM, respectively); 13, PBS (10mM); 14–20, trypsin, thrombin, ribonuclease, metalloproteinase 1, metalloproteinase 2, lysozyme and HAase (400U/mL, respectively).

Confocal fluorescence microscopy images, (a) HeLa cells treated with P-CDs/HA-Dox for 2h at 37°C. (b) Hela cells pretreated with 9mg/mL HA for 30min and then treated with P-CDs/HA-Dox for 2h at 37°C and (c) NIH-3T3 cells incubated with P-CDs/HA-Dox for 2h at 37°C. (d) Relative pixel intensity of the corresponding fluorescence images in panels 1 and 2 (the pixel intensity from the HeLa cells incubated with the P-CDs/HA-Dox was defined as 1.0).
Cytotoxic effects of CDs, P-CDs, P-CDs/HA-Dox, P-CDs/HA and Dox on (a) HeLa cells and (b) NIH-3T3 cells.
Schematic illustration of the formation of PEI-CDs/HA-Dox, and the nanoprobe used for targeted cancer cell imaging and drug delivery.

head.ce:title: Turn-on theranostic fluorescent nanoprobe by electrostatic self-assembly of carbon dots with doxorubicin for targeted cancer cell imaging, in vivo hyaluronidase analysis, and targeted drug delivery

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry & Chemical Engineering, Shaanxi Key Laboratory of Chemical Reaction Engineering, Yan’an University, Yan’an, Shaanxi 716000, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry & Chemical Engineering, Shaanxi Key Laboratory of Chemical Reaction Engineering, Yan’an University

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Cluster Science of Ministry of Education, Beijing Key Laboratory of Photoelectronic/Electrophotonic Conversion Materials, School of Chemistry, Beijing Institute of Technology, Beijing 100081, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Cluster Science of Ministry of Education, Beijing Key Laboratory of Photoelectronic/Electrophotonic Conversion Materials, School of Chemistry, Beijing Institute of Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This paper reports a turn-on theranostic fluorescent nanoprobe P-CDs/HA-Dox obtained by electrostatic assembly of polyethylenimine (PEI)-modified carbon dots (P-CDs) and Hyaluronic acid (HA)-conjugated doxorubicin (Dox) for hyaluronidase (HAase) detection, self-targeted imaging and drug delivery. P-CDs/HA-Dox show weak emission in a physiological environment. By utilizing the high affinity of HA to CD44 receptors overexpressed on many cancer cells, P-CDs/HA-Dox are capable of targeting and penetrating into cancer cells, where they are activated by HAase. As a result, HA-Dox can be digested into small fragments, causing the release of Dox and thereby restoring the fluorescence of P-CDs. The theranostic fluorescent nanoprobe can effectively distinguish cancer cells from normal cells. The as-prepared nanoprobe achieves a sensitive assay of HAase with a detection limit of 0.65UmL−1. Furthermore, upon Dox release, the Dox could efficiently induce apoptosis in HeLa cells, as confirmed by MTT assay. The design of such a turn-on theranostic fluorescent probe provides a new strategy for self-targeted and image-guided chemotherapy.

ce:section.ce:para: Hyaluronic acid (HA) is a negatively charged polysaccharide made of repeating disaccharide units, N-acetyl-D-glucosamine and D-glucuronic acid (Lapcik et al., 1998; Lokeshwar et al., 2001). It is abundant in the extracellular matrix and synovial fluids of all vertebrates and participates in several biological processes such as tissue hydration, and cell motility, proliferation and differentiation (Toole and Slomiany, 2008). Importantly, HA exhibits a high specific affinity for CD44 receptors overexpressed on the surfaces of many cancer cells, and can enter cells through receptor-mediated endocytosis (Kim et al., 2010). Hyaluronidase (HAase), a family of endoglycosidases, can degrade HA by cleaving its internal β-N-acetyl-D-glucosamine bonds (Lokeshwara and Selzer, 2008). It has been reported that HAase has a close relationship with the presence of many malignant tumors (Wang et al., 2015). The expressed levels of HAase greatly increase in cancer cells, whereas HAase is less distributed in normal cells (Lokeshwar et al., 1996). Therefore, it is of considerable importance to construct a theranostic fluorescent probe for the evaluation of HAase level, and subsequently, in vivo drug delivery and cancer diagnosis and therapy (Bhang et al., 2009; Chen et al., 2015; Han et al., 2016; Ma et al., 2012; Swierczewska et al., 2012).

Carbon dots (CDs) represent a new class of biocompatible fluorescent carbon materials for various biological applications because of their fascinating properties, such as small sizes, good aqueous solubility and tunable optical properties by optimized precursors and synthetic procedure (Ge et al., 2015; Gong et al., 2016; Shen et al., 2012). A number of studies have explored CDs as fluorescent nanoprobes for intracellular imaging (Goh et al., 2012; Hsu and Chang, 2012; Kong et al., 2012; Liu et al., 2009; Zhao et al., 2015). However, most of these methods are either “fluorescence always on” or “fluorescence turn-off” due to non-specific endocytosis or nonradiative electron transfer between carbon dots and target molecules. It is well known that “fluorescence always on” nanoprobes and “turn-off” nanoprobes may either lead to false positive signals or suffer potential interference from the environment, which may be problematic for utilization in quantitative measurements (B.Y. Feng et al., 2013; Verma and Stellacci, 2010; Zhang et al., 2009). In contrast, “turn-on fluorescence” nanoprobes, whose signals is activated only by the targeted molecule or cancer cell, not only address the above-mentioned problems, but also enhance optical signal in microscopy (D. Feng et al., 2013; Pan et al., 2013; Silvers et al., 2012; Wang et al., 2013). Thus, it is desirable to construct turn-on fluorescent CD-based nanoprobe for targeted cells detection. Moreover, it is great interesting to design and construct a multifunctional CDs-based composite that combines diagnostic, self-targeting, and drug-delivery functions to achieve accurate treatment and avoid drug side reaction, especially in anticancer treatment (Gao et al., 2016; Wójcik et al., 2015).

The electrostatic self-assembly method provides a facile and versatile strategy to build multi-functional CDs composites by cyclic adsorption of charged polyelectrolyte and oppositely charged CDs (Caruso et al., 1998; Caruso and Mohwald, 1999; Medley et al., 2008; Zhao et al., 2013). This strategy makes electrostatic self-assembly of CDs-based composites containing doxorubicin (Dox) modified HA suitable for constructing turn-on theranostic fluorescent nanoprobe for the evaluation of HAase levels, and for cancer cell treatment. We have developed a turn-on theranostic fluorescent nanoprobe, In which, CDs, Dox, and HA are designed as fluorescent agent, energy acceptor, and cell-targeting moiety, respectively (Fig. S1). First, polyethylenimine modified CDs (P-CDs) emit strong fluorescence, and its fluorescence intensity is insusceptible of biological environments. Second, Doxorubicin (Dox), a selected chemotherapy medication model, interacts with DNA by binding to a DNA-associated enzyme and intercalating in the DNA pair, and it also inhibits macromolecular biosynthesis (Tacar et al., 2012; Yang et al., 2016). Moreover, Tang et al. reported an efficient Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) process between CDs and Dox (Tang et al., 2013). Third, HA is a typical cancer-cell targeting agent because it can bind to several different cancer cell receptors, including CD44. In addition, Lokeshwar et al. showed that HAase significantly elevation (3–10 fold) in tumor tissues compared to the normal adult prostate (Lokeshwar et al., 1996). The level of HAase in different cells allows us to self-target and distinguish cancer cells from normal cell. To the best our knowledge, this type of multi-functional CDs-based composites has not been reported previously.

Scheme 1 depicts the design of the electrostatic self-assembly of the multi-functional CDs-based composite and its fluorescence turn-on response to HAase in cancer cells. First, CDs are modified with a positively charged polyelectrolyte, PEI via electrostatic interaction, and then functionalized P-CDs with HA-Dox (reaction product of HA with Dox conventional EDC chemistry). The theranostic fluorescent nanoprobe produces a negligible fluorescence signal due to the Förster resonance energy transfer from P-CDs to Dox. As shown in Scheme 1, the theranostic fluorescent nanoprobe could be activated by HAase in cancer cells, The HA can be extensively digested into tetrasaccharides or small fragments in the presence of HAase, resulting in release of Dox for drug delivery, and subsequent recovery of fluorescence of P-CDs. Furthermore, the release of Dox can efficiently induce apoptosis in HeLa cells, as confirmed by the MTT assay.

All materials are purchased and used without further purification (see Supporting information). In a typical synthesis, 0.5g ATP was mixed with 10mL deionized water to form a clear solution. Then the mixture was heated hydrothermally in a Nabertherm Muffle furnace (Germany) at 300°C for 4h to form the CDs. After the light yellow solution cooled to room temperature, CDs were obtained by filtering through a 0.22µm membrane. The CDs were then dialysed several times against deionized water with stirring. Finally, 1mL of CDs solution, 30μL of PEI and 4mL of phosphate buffered saline (PBS, pH 7.4) were mixed and reacted for 3h at 60°C to form the PEI-CDs.

First, HA (50mg, 0.012mmol) was dispersed in 10mL of a mixture of DMSO and H2O. Then, 0.07mmol of EDC (20mg/mL) and 0.16mmol NHS (aq) (10mg/mL) were added to the HA solution, followed by stirring for about 30min. Subsequently, 0.0086mmol of Dox·HCl (aq) (5mg/mL) was added, and the mixture was further stirred at room temperature for 24h. Unbound Dox·HCl, EDC, and NHS were removed by dialyzing the resultant solution against deionized water for 3 days. The HA-Dox samples were lyophilized as a solid and stored at −20°C in the absence of light.

The Dox loading efficiency (DLE) was detected by UV–vis absorbance. The absorbance of Dox was measured at 480nm and the amount of Dox was calculated using the standard calibration curve.
DLE
( % ) = ( amount
of
Dox
conjugated
to
carriers /
total
amount
of
Dox
in
the
reaction ) × 100

For the detection of HAase, initially, the HA-Dox and P-CDs solutions were mixed at the desired concentrations to form P-CDs/HA-Dox. Then, the precipitate was collected by centrifugation of the resultant solution to remove unbound HA-Dox. HAase was dissolved in PBS (0.02M, pH 5.6) solution for the subsequent experiment. Different concentrations of HAase (1–10μL, 800U/mL) were respectively added to P-CDs/HA-Dox, and then 200μL of PBS was added. After incubation at 37°C for 40min, the mixed solution was diluted to 1mL with PBS buffer. The fluorescence was measured with an excitation wavelength of 310nm.

HeLa cells (human cervical cancer cells) and NIH-3T3 cells (normal mice fibroblast cells) were selected to demonstrate that P-CDs/HA-Dox were capable of targeted cancer cell imaging. The cells were seeded in confocal microscope dishes the day before the experiments to allow the cells to adhere to the plate. The medium was then replaced with the medium containing the P-CDs/HA-Dox. The final concentrations of P-CDs and HA-Dox were 2.6×10−4 M and 5.2×10−5 M, respectively. After incubation for 2h at 37°C, the cells were washed three times with phosphate buffered saline (PBS, 0.01M, pH=7.4). In addition, before treatment with the P-CDs/HA-Dox, another batch of HeLa cells was incubated with a high concentration of HA (9mg/mL) for 30min at 37°C as a control experiment. The fluorescence images of P-CDs and Dox were conducted by a confocal laser scanning microscope with excitation wavelength at 405nm and 488nm, and the fluorescence wavelengths recorded in the ranges of 450–500nm and 550–600nm, respectively.

In vitro cytotoxicity of CDs, P-CDs and the P-CDs/HA-Dox composite was evaluated by the MTT assay. Cells were seeded (5×103 mL−1) in 96-well plates and incubated in Dulbecco modified Eagle medium (DMEM) containing 10% fetal bovine serum (FBS) and 1% penicillin–streptomycin at 37°C in a 5% CO2 incubator for 24h. Culture medium was removed and the medium was then replaced with DMEM containing various concentrations of test solutions for another 24h. At the end of the incubation, 10μL MTT (5.0mg/mL) was added and cultured for another 4h. Then the growth medium was removed and 100μL of DMSO was added to each well to dissolve MTT. Finally, the absorbance of each sample was measured at 570nm on a microplate reader (Bio-Rad 680, USA).

The successful assembly of P-CDs/HA-Dox was characterized via transmission electron microscopy (TEM), atomic force microscopy (AFM) and zeta potential measurements. As shown in Fig. 1a, the CDs were monodispersed and exhibited a crystalline structure with a lattice spacing value of 0.27nm, which corresponds to the (020) planes of graphitic carbon (Chen et al., 2015). The diameter of CDs was calculated to be in the range of 1.4–3.2nm with an average diameter of 2.2nm (Fig. S2). After PEI modification, the formed P-CDs generally retained good dispersibility (Fig. 1b). Subsequently, the further assembly of HA-Dox onto the P-CDs generated large aggregates (Fig. 1c), indicating the successful preparation of P-CDs/HA-Dox via electrostatic interaction. As shown in Fig. S3, the diameters of P-CDs/HA-Dox are distributed in the range of 15–30nm with an average diameter of 20nm. The AFM images (Fig. S4) showed that CDs, P-CDs and P-CDs/HA-Dox had gradually increasing diameters and remained as individual particles. These results were consistent with the TEM characterization. The combination between PEI-CDs and HA-Dox was further verified by the measurement of zeta potential. As shown in Fig. 1d, the charge of CDs increased from −5.36mV to +4.96mV upon PEI modification, which was attributed to the introduction of positively-charged amine groups. The zeta potential of P-CDs/HA-Dox was −24.1mV, indicating electrostatic attraction between the positively-charged P-CDs and HA-Dox (−50.7mV).

The absorption and emission spectra of CDs, P-CDs, and P-CDs/HA-Dox were first investigated. In the UV–vis spectra ( Fig. 2a), the CDs showed three typical absorption bands around 240nm, 280nm and 310nm, which were assigned to the π→π* transition of the aromatic ring, n→π* transition of C=O and the trapping of excited-state energy by the surface states, respectively (Zhang et al., 2014; Zhang and Chen, 2014). After assembly of HA-Dox onto PEI-CDs, the characteristic absorption around 480nm for Dox appeared in P-CDs/HA-Dox, further demonstrating the combination between HA-Dox and P-CDs. As shown in Fig. S5a, P-CDs emitted bright fluorescence and displayed the same emission band centered at 380nm as the CDs. The emission peak of P-CDs redshifted from 370nm to 450nm as the excitation wavelength increased from 290nm to 370nm (Fig. S5b). Its excitation wavelength-dependent fluorescence feature was similar to that of carbon materials, and could be attributed to emissive traps and the electronic conjugate structure of CDs (Dong et al., 2010; Jin et al., 2015; Li et al., 2011). As shown in Figs. 2b and 3a, upon HA-Dox addition, the P-CDs fluorescence was quenched gradually and decreased to about 24% of the initial value. The fluorescence quenching mechanism between P-CDs and Dox in P-CDs/HA-Dox was further studied. As shown in Fig. S6, the absorption spectrum of Dox overlaps the emission spectrum of P-CDs. Moreover, with the introduction of increasing concentrations of Dox, the fluorescence intensity of P-CDs gradually decreases, while the fluorescence intensity at the emission peak of Dox progressively increases (Fig. S7). This phenomenon indicates that the FRET process from P-CDs to Dox has occurred (Tang et al., 2013).

The suitability of P-CDs/HA-Dox for HAase detection was studied in vitro. As shown in Fig. 3c, the successive addition of HAase into the P-CDs/HA-Dox solution led to a progressive recovery in fluorescence intensity. In the presence of HAase, the FRET between P-CDs and HA-Dox became weaker due to the hydrolysis of HA. When this occurred, Dox was released from the HA-Dox and further separated from P-CDs, led to the recovery of P-CDs fluorescence. The fluorescence intensity showed a 6-fold enhancement and has an excellent linear relationship (R2=0.9919) with HAase concentration of up to 400UmL−1 (Fig. 3d). The detection limit for HAase was 0.65UmL−1 calculated by a signal-to-noise ratio of 3. Therefore, P-CDs/HA-Dox showed a great potential for HAase detection with high sensitivity.

To confirm that the fluorescence recovery was specifically caused by HAase, we studied the fluorescence response of P-CDs/HA-Dox toward various biologically significant substances, such as NaCl, KCl, MgCl2 and NaH2PO4, ascorbic acid, glucose, urea, glutathione, cysteine, homocysteine, histidine, PBS, trypsin, thrombin, ribonuclease, metalloproteinase 1, metalloproteinase 2, lysozyme and HAase. As displayed in Fig. 4, the fluorescence intensity at 380nm increased by around 6-fold in the presence of HAase, but showed negligible changes in the presence of other substances. Except for HAase, other enzymes have only a slight effect on the fluorescence intensity of the nanoprobe P-CDs/HA-Dox. These results suggested that the P-CDs/HA-Dox nanoprobe enabled the selective detection of HAase under physiological conditions.

The effects of acidity and ionic strength on the fluorescence behavior of P-CDs were also explored. The florescence intensity remained stable level in the pH range of 4–12 (Fig. S9a). In PBS solution containing different concentrations of NaCl, the fluorescence showed negligible variation with increasing ionic strength (Fig. S9b). During UV irradiation for 30min, the fluorescence intensity of P-CDs was quenched to a low level and remained stable in the presence of HA-Dox (400μM) (Fig. S10). All these findings revealed that P-CDs/HA-Dox suffered weak interferences from changes in environmental factors including pH, ionic strength and irradiation.

CD44 is a cell glycoprotein, which has been identified as an HA receptor (Nano et al., 1997). Aberrant expression of CD44 is associated with metastatic spread and poor prognosis in human malignancies. Moreover, it is very important to distinguish cancer cells from normal cells using a nanoprobe such as CD44 and its variants, which have been used as diagnostic or prognostic markers for at least some human malignant diseases (Driel et al., 1998). For example, Al-Hajj et al. were able to distinguish tumorigenic (tumor initiating) from the nontumorigenic cancer cells based on cell surface marker expression, and they have identified and isolated the tumorigenic cells as CD44+ CD24-/low Lineage- cell (Al-Hajj et al., 2003).Therefore, human cervical cancer Hela cells (CD44 high expression) and normal mice fibroblast NIH-3T3 cells (CD44 low expression) were employed as model cells to examine the cancer-targetable ability of our nanoprobe (Chong et al., 2013; Twomey et al., 2013). As shown in Fig. 5, we found that P-CDs/HA-Dox was capable of targeting and penetrating into cancer cells by utilizing the high affinity of HA to CD44 receptors. Upon P-CDs/HA-Dox incubation, HeLa cells clearly exhibited fluorescence signals in the blue and red channels (Fig. 5a), which were assigned for fluorescence collection from P-CDs and Dox, respectively. This phenomenon demonstrated that P-CDs/HA-Dox had internalized into Hela cells, where the HA-Dox chain was decomposed and separated from P-CDs, resulting in fluorescence recovery of P-CDs, and the release of Dox. In contrast to Hela cells, NIH-3T3 cells emitted extremely faint fluorescence in both channels (Fig. 5c), indicating the weak penetration of P-CDs/HA-Dox, and the fluorescence of CDs and Dox did not restored due to the efficient Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) process between CDs and Dox. To explore the cellular uptake mechanism of P-CDs/HA-Dox, HeLa cells pre-treated with anti-CD44 antibody to block CD44 receptors were used as control cells to be incubated with the nanoprobe. As seen in Fig. S11, the cells emit only faint fluorescence in both channels. These results implied that P-CDs/HA-Dox was internalized into cells mainly by CD44-mediated endocytosis. Based on intracellular fluorescence imaging experiment, P-CDs/HA-Dox was found to act as nanoprobe for distinguishing cancer cells from normal cells.

The cellular hyaluronidase (HAase) level is elevated in many carcinomas, and its expression in cancer cells is correlated with tumor invasiveness. P-CDs/HA-Dox could also be applied as a nanoprobe for evaluating the relative content of HAase in HeLa cells. As depicted in Fig. 5b, HeLa cells pretreated with high doses of free HA emitted slight fluorescence in the blue and red channels, may be due to the consumption of intracellular HAase. To assess changes in HAase level, relative pixel intensity analysis was carried out using ImageJ software (Li et al., 2014). The results (Fig. 5d) showed that the fluorescent intensity of HeLa cells incubated with P-CDs/HA-Dox was about 2.5 times (P-CDs) and 5 times (Dox) higher than that of HA-treated HeLa cells, indicating the significant role of HAase in the fluorescence recovery of P-CDs and the releasing of Dox. Thus, the nanoprobe displayed great potential for HAase detection in cancer cells.

HAase-activated self-targeted drug delivery is also important for effective treatment of cancer and to avoid side reactions caused by anticancer drugs. Therefore, it is highly desirable to develop an analysis strategy and theranostic platform to evaluate the relative levels of HAase in cancer cells, and to achieve in vivo targeted drug delivery and chemotherapy. P-CDs/HA-Dox could serve as a drug delivery platform for targeted cancer therapy. To investigate the treatment effect, cytotoxicity assay was carried out by MTT measurement in cancer HeLa cells and normal NIH-3T3 cells. As shown in Fig. 6a, the nanoprobe P-CDs/HA-Dox shows effective treatment for cancer HeLa cells even at low concentration (0.5–2.5μg/mL). The drug loading rate of the nanoprobe is calculated to be about 6.3%. However, upon exposure to a higher concentration (50μg/mL) of free Dox, only 20% of HeLa cells are killed. P-CDs/HA-Dox exhibits higher cytotoxicity than free Dox owing to its good ability of targeted drug delivery. Unlike HeLa cells, 90% and 50% of the normal NIH-3T3 cells are able to survive upon incubation with a high concentration (50μg/mL) of P-CDs/HA-Dox and free Dox (Fig. 6b). Compared with free Dox, P-CDs/HA-Dox presents lower cytotoxicity, because the lack of CD44 receptors on NIH-3T3 cell surface can inhibit the cellular uptake of P-CDs/HA-Dox. Generally, the drug delivery platform of P-CDs/HA-Dox with excellent cancer targeting exhibits higher cytotoxicity for cancer cells but lower side effects for normal cells. Moreover, CDs, P-CDs and P-CDs/HA complex display limited effect on cell viability, indicating their good biocompatibility. The MTT results showed that CDs, P-CDs and P-CDs/HA exhibit very low cytotoxicity at the tested dose, but the composite caused significant damage to CD44-overexpressed HeLa cells, indicating that the P-CDs/HA–Dox nanoprobe has great potential in cancer therapy.

A well-designed turn-on fluorescent nanoprobe, P-CDs/HA-Dox, has been successfully constructed by integrating P-CDs with HA-Dox through electrostatic self-assembly. As-prepared P-CDs/HA-Dox exhibited excellent colloidal stability, good biocompatibility, and targeting specificity to CD44 receptors overexpressed on cancer cells. Moreover, under the activation of HAase, P-CDs/HA-Dox restores the fluorescence of P-CDs leading to enzyme-triggered drug release. Therefore, as-prepared P-CDs/HA-Dox could achieve a sensitive assay of HAase with a detection limit of 0.65UmL−1. The MTT assay confirmed that the release of Dox from the turn-on fluorescent nanoprobe can efficiently induce apoptosis in HeLa cells. Both in vitro and in vivo experiments showed that the combination of targeted bioimaging, hyaluronidase analysis, and targeted drug delivery through electrostatic assembly P-CDs and HA-Dox offer a synergistic improvement in both cancer diagnosis and therapeutic efficiency compared with DOX anticancer drug alone. Therefore, the fluorescent nanoprobe is a very promising theranostic strategy for non-invasive self-targeted and image-guided chemotherapy.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We gratefully acknowledge financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 21575015 and 21505004), Beijing National Laboratory for Molecular Sciences (BNLMS) (20140121), the 111 Project (B07012) and the Natural Science Foundation of Beijing Municipality (No. 2172051) for financial support. This research work was supported by the Open Funds of the State Key Laboratory of Electroanalytical Chemistry (SKLEAC201708).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.bios.2017.05.019.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
Supplementary material
.

Supplementary material
.
